verdict: Spending is producing some activity, but very little of it is turning into booked work. Over the last 28 days, $2,115.77 in Instagram ads generated 9 leads through the platform's form, which works out to roughly $235 per lead — at the high end of what HVAC operators typically pay. Meanwhile, the local presence on Google (107 new reviews this period, all five stars) is the real engine driving phone calls and direction requests. The biggest risk right now: the customer pipeline shows $0 in opportunity value, which means leads are coming in but no one is being moved toward a booked job.
diagnosis: The Instagram campaign "AC Installation and Replacement" is reaching people (78,716 impressions, 64,852 unique people reached) but the path from interest to booked job is leaking badly. Out of 587 clicks, only 86 were actual link clicks to your website, and the campaign produced 9 leads through the on-platform form at an average cost of about $235 each. That sits well above the typical $60–$229 range cited for HVAC businesses this year and is also above the roughly $144 average cost per lead reported for non-branded HVAC search campaigns industry-wide. On the bright side, your Google Business Profile is doing heavy lifting for free: 24 phone-call taps, 44 direction requests, 107 new reviews this period averaging 5 stars, and 934 total profile views across mobile and desktop search and maps. The disconnect: the customer pipeline in the CRM shows opportunities being created (one named contact came in from Facebook, another from your website form) but every single opportunity has a monetary value of $0 and status of "open." That suggests leads are landing but no one is qualifying, pricing, or following up in a way that turns them into actual revenue. Industry research consistently points to response speed as the single biggest lever — 78% of homeowners hire the first company that gets back to them.

signals.headline: Cost per lead from Instagram is running roughly $235
signals.detail: Spend of $2,115.77 produced 9 form leads this period. That's noticeably above the $60–$229 range cited for HVAC businesses in 2026, and well above the $144 average reported for non-branded HVAC search campaigns. The good news: the latest day shows a cost per lead of only $1.37, suggesting recent ad adjustments may be improving efficiency.

signals.headline: Reviews are exploding and customers love the work
signals.detail: 107 new reviews came in this period with an average rating of 5 stars. One recent reviewer specifically called out a complete AC and furnace replacement job as 'excellent,' 'professional,' and 'meticulous.' This is a serious competitive asset that most local HVAC competitors don't have.

signals.headline: The Google Business Profile is outperforming the paid ads
signals.detail: Without spending a dollar, your local listing produced 24 phone-call taps, 44 direction requests, 91 website clicks, and was shown 934 times across mobile and desktop search and maps. For a local services business, this is the highest-intent traffic you have.

signals.headline: The customer pipeline shows $0 in opportunity value
signals.detail: Leads are being created in the CRM (one from Facebook, one from the website contact form) but every opportunity is sitting open with no dollar amount attached. This means either no one is quoting these leads or quotes aren't being logged — both are revenue leaks.

signals.headline: Instagram engagement is thin compared to the reach
signals.detail: The account has 166 followers, just 8 new follower additions this period, 5 profile visits, and 14 total interactions across 98 posts. The ads are reaching people but the organic profile isn't converting that attention into ongoing audience.

benchmark.label: Industry benchmarks
benchmark.subheader: What HVAC operators typically pay for a lead in 2026
benchmark.body: Multiple 2026 industry reports place the cost per lead for HVAC businesses in a wide band — roughly $60 to $229 for standard residential HVAC work, with non-branded paid search campaigns averaging around $144 per lead and general HVAC campaigns averaging about $198 per lead. AC repair campaigns specifically run higher, around $231 per lead. Your current Instagram cost per lead of roughly $235 sits at the top of that range, though your latest-day figure of $1.37 suggests strong improvement is possible. On Facebook and Instagram specifically, conversion rates for home improvement services are projected to land around 5.5% in 2026, and lead-focused ad campaigns typically see click-through rates around 2.5%.

seasonal.label: Seasonal context
seasonal.subheader: Late May is the start of the most expensive — and most lucrative — season
seasonal.body: The HVAC industry sees cost-per-click spikes in summer (cooling) and winter (heating), and conversion rates climb sharply when urgency rises (heatwaves, broken units). High-intent searches like 'AC repair near me' or 'emergency HVAC' can hit $22–$40 per click in competitive markets. The window from late May through August is when homeowners stop putting off AC replacement decisions, so budget pressure and lead volume both rise together. This is the right month to be aggressive — but only if the back-end (response time, quoting, follow-up) can handle the volume.

competitor.label: Competitive landscape
competitor.subheader: Private-equity-backed HVAC consolidators are bidding up local ad costs
competitor.body: The HVAC industry recorded 149 mergers and acquisitions in the most recent tracked period — a 12.9% increase year-over-year — with private equity firms buying up local operators and rolling them up into regional brands. These backed competitors have deeper pockets and are willing to overpay for leads in the short term to capture market share. For a local Crestwood-area operator, this means rising ad costs are likely to continue, and the defensible advantages are the things money can't buy quickly: a strong review reputation (which you have — 5-star average across 107 new reviews this period), fast phone response, and a recognizable local name.

recommendations.title: Consider fixing the lead-to-booked-job pipeline before adding more ad budget
recommendations.detail: Every opportunity in the CRM this period is showing $0 in value and 'open' status. Before spending more on Instagram, it's worth auditing what happens after a lead form is submitted: Who calls them? How fast? Is a quote being attached to the opportunity record? Industry data consistently shows that responding within 60 seconds dramatically increases the chance of winning the job, while waiting even five minutes cuts qualification rates by 80%. Test a simple change — assign one person to call every new lead within 10 minutes during business hours — and measure whether the number of opportunities with a real dollar value goes up over the next 28 days.

recommendations.title: Consider leaning harder into the Google Business Profile, which is already your best free channel
recommendations.detail: Without paid spend, your local listing produced 24 phone taps, 44 direction requests, and 91 website clicks this period, plus pulled in 107 new five-star reviews. Worth exploring: posting weekly updates on the profile (you have one recent post about honest recommendations — that's a strong angle), adding photos of completed installations, and asking every happy customer for a review with a direct link. Each review compounds your visibility in local search and maps, where homeowners with broken AC units are looking right now.

recommendations.title: Test a tighter Instagram ad that sends people to a faster booking path
recommendations.detail: The current ad 'Is your AC ready for summers?' is broad-targeted and routes through a free-estimate form. With 587 clicks producing only 9 leads through the platform's form, the drop-off is significant. Worth testing: a more specific offer (e.g., a fixed-price tune-up or a financing-focused message for replacements), paired with a click-to-call button instead of, or alongside, the form. Home improvement ads on Meta are projected to convert at around 5.5% in 2026, and a click-to-call route would also let you measure phone leads directly. Keep spend at roughly current levels while testing — don't scale until the cost-per-booked-job math works.
